snapshot.source_text: Geometry Optimization and Energy Calculation. The molecules and transition states were optimized at the gradient- corrected DFT level using the exchange functional of Becke27 and the correlation functional of Perdew28 (BP86). Relativistic effects were considered using the zeroth-order regular ap- proximation (ZORA).29 Uncontracted Slater-type orbitals (STOs) were used as basis functions for the SCF calculations.30 The basis functions at the metal have triple- \xi quality, augmented with a set of 6p functions. The basis set at the other atoms has double- \xi quality, augmented with a set of d-type polarization functions. The (1s)2 core electrons of C and O and the \mathrm { ( 1 s 2 s 2 p 3 s 3 p 3 d 4 s 4 p 4 d ) ^ { 4 6 } } inner shells of Re were treated within the frozen-core approximation.31 An auxiliary basis set of s, p, d, f, and g STOs was utilized to fit the molecular densities and

item.translation_unit_protected_source_text: Geometry Optimization and Energy Calculation. The molecules and transition states were optimized at the gradient- corrected DFT level using the exchange functional of Becke27 and the correlation functional of Perdew28 (BP86). Relativistic effects were considered using the zeroth-order regular ap- proximation (ZORA).29 Uncontracted Slater-type orbitals (STOs) were used as basis functions for the SCF calculations.30 The basis functions at the metal have triple- \xi quality, augmented with a set of 6p functions. The basis set at the other atoms has double- \xi quality, augmented with a set of d-type polarization functions. The (1s)2 core electrons of C and O and the \mathrm { ( 1 s 2 s 2 p 3 s 3 p 3 d 4 s 4 p 4 d ) ^ { 4 6 } } inner shells of Re were treated within the frozen-core approximation.31 An auxiliary basis set of s, p, d, f, and g STOs was utilized to fit the molecular densities and to represent the Coulomb and exchange potentials in each SCF cycle.32 This basis-set combination is denoted \mathrm { I I I } \sim . 22,23 Unless otherwise stated, energies reported refer to the BP86/III∼ level. The calculations were carried out using the ADF 2000 program package.34

item.group_protected_source_text: Geometry Optimization and Energy Calculation. The molecules and transition states were optimized at the gradient- corrected DFT level using the exchange functional of Becke27 and the correlation functional of Perdew28 (BP86). Relativistic effects were considered using the zeroth-order regular ap- proximation (ZORA).29 Uncontracted Slater-type orbitals (STOs) were used as basis functions for the SCF calculations.30 The basis functions at the metal have triple- \xi quality, augmented with a set of 6p functions. The basis set at the other atoms has double- \xi quality, augmented with a set of d-type polarization functions. The (1s)2 core electrons of C and O and the \mathrm { ( 1 s 2 s 2 p 3 s 3 p 3 d 4 s 4 p 4 d ) ^ { 4 6 } } inner shells of Re were treated within the frozen-core approximation.31 An auxiliary basis set of s, p, d, f, and g STOs was utilized to fit the molecular densities and to represent the Coulomb and exchange potentials in each SCF cycle.32 This basis-set combination is denoted \mathrm { I I I } \sim . 22,23 Unless otherwise stated, energies reported refer to the BP86/III∼ level. The calculations were carried out using the ADF 2000 program package.34

page_payload.source_text: Organic molecules with double bonds, such as ethylene and formaldehyde, are well-known to undergo [ 2 + 2] cycloadditions across the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { C } + moiety of ketene. Surprisingly, the same pathway was recently predicted to be kinetically favored in the reaction of \mathrm { C p R e O } _ { 3 } with ketene, in contrast to the additions of other metal oxides to the heterocumulene. Since the origin of the activation energies of [ 2 + 2 ] cycloadditions was poorly understood, a comparative density-functional study on the [ 2 + 2 ] + addition of \mathrm { X = Y } \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } { = } \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } , \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } { = } \mathrm { O } , and \mathrm { C p O } _ { 2 } \mathrm { R e } { = } \mathrm { O } ) across the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { C } + bond of ketene has been carried out. The electronic structure of the transition states has been analyzed in terms of interactions between the ketene and \mathrm { X = Y } fragments. The analysis reveals that, besides stabilizing orbital interactions, additional contributions such as electrostatics and repulsion due to the Pauli principle determine the activation energies of cycloadditions. The stabilization arising from orbital interactions is weakest in the transition state for the metal oxide addition to the heterocumulene, although this reaction has the lowest activation barrier.

Surprisingly, the same pathway was recently predicted to be kinetically favored in the reaction of \mathrm { C p R e O } _ { 3 } with ketene, in contrast to the additions of other metal oxides to the heterocumulene. Since the origin of the activation energies of [ 2 + 2 ] cycloadditions was poorly understood, a comparative density-functional study on the [ 2 + 2 ] + addition of \mathrm { X = Y } \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } { = } \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } , \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } { = } \mathrm { O } , and \mathrm { C p O } _ { 2 } \mathrm { R e } { = } \mathrm { O } ) across the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { C } + bond of ketene has been carried out. The electronic structure of the transition states has been analyzed in terms of interactions between the ketene and \mathrm { X = Y } fragments. The analysis reveals that, besides stabilizing orbital interactions, additional contributions such as electrostatics and repulsion due to the Pauli principle determine the activation energies of cycloadditions. The stabilization arising from orbital interactions is weakest in the transition state for the metal oxide addition to the heterocumulene, although this reaction has the lowest activation barrier.

page_payload.translation_unit_protected_source_text: Organic molecules with double bonds, such as ethylene and formaldehyde, are well-known to undergo [ 2 + 2] cycloadditions across the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { C } + moiety of ketene. Surprisingly, the same pathway was recently predicted to be kinetically favored in the reaction of \mathrm { C p R e O } _ { 3 } with ketene, in contrast to the additions of other metal oxides to the heterocumulene. Since the origin of the activation energies of [ 2 + 2 ] cycloadditions was poorly understood, a comparative density-functional study on the [ 2 + 2 ] + addition of \mathrm { X = Y } \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } { = } \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } , \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } { = } \mathrm { O } , and \mathrm { C p O } _ { 2 } \mathrm { R e } { = } \mathrm { O } ) across the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { C } + bond of ketene has been carried out. The electronic structure of the transition states has been analyzed in terms of interactions between the ketene and \mathrm { X = Y } fragments. The analysis reveals that, besides stabilizing orbital interactions, additional contributions such as electrostatics and repulsion due to the Pauli principle determine the activation energies of cycloadditions. The stabilization arising from orbital interactions is weakest in the transition state for the metal oxide addition to the heterocumulene, although this reaction has the lowest activation barrier.
page_payload.translation_unit_protected_translated_te: Organic molecules containing double bonds, such as ethylene and formaldehyde, are known to undergo [2+2] cycloaddition reactions with the C=C+ portion of ketene. Surprisingly, recent predictions suggest that the reaction of CpReO$_{3}$ with ketene also proceeds through the same kinetically more favorable pathway, which contrasts with the addition reactions of other metal oxides to this heterocumulene. Due to insufficient understanding of the origin of activation energies for [2+2] cycloaddition reactions, we conducted a comparative density functional study examining the [2+2+] additions of X=Y (CH$_{2}$=CH$_{2}$, CH$_{2}$=O and CpO$_{2}$Re=O) with the C=C+ bond of ketene. From the ketene and X=Yhrm{X=Y}$Segment to analyze the electronic structure of the transition state from the perspective of the interaction between segments. The analysis shows that, in addition to stable orbital interactions, extra contributions such as electrostatic interactions and repulsion due to the Pauli principle also determine the activation energies of the cycloaddition reactions. Although the reactions of metal oxides and heterocumulenes have the lowest activation barriers, the stabilization from orbital interactions in their transition states is the weakest.
page_payload.translation_unit_translated_text: Organic molecules containing double bonds, such as ethylene and formaldehyde, are known to undergo [2+2] cycloaddition reactions with the C=C+ portion of ketene. Surprisingly, recent predictions suggest that the reaction of CpReO$_{3}$ with ketene also proceeds through the same kinetically more favorable pathway, which contrasts with the addition reactions of other metal oxides to this heterocumulene. Due to insufficient understanding of the origin of activation energies for [2+2] cycloaddition reactions, we conducted a comparative density functional study examining the [2+2+] additions of X=Y (CH$_{2}$=CH$_{2}$, CH$_{2}$=O and CpO$_{2}$Re=O) with the C=C+ bond of ketene. From the ketene and X=Yhrm{X=Y}$Segment to analyze the electronic structure of the transition state from the perspective of the interaction between segments. The analysis shows that, in addition to stable orbital interactions, extra contributions such as electrostatic interactions and repulsion due to the Pauli principle also determine the activation energies of the cycloaddition reactions. Although the reactions of metal oxides and heterocumulenes have the lowest activation barriers, the stabilization from orbital interactions in their transition states is the weakest.

page_payload.translated_text: Organic molecules containing double bonds, such as ethylene and formaldehyde, are known to undergo [2+2] cycloaddition reactions with the C=C+ portion of ketene. Surprisingly, recent predictions suggest that the reaction of CpReO$_{3}$ with ketene also proceeds through the same kinetically more favorable pathway, which contrasts with the addition reactions of other metal oxides to this heterocumulene. Due to insufficient understanding of the origin of activation energies for [2+2] cycloaddition reactions, we conducted a comparative density functional study examining the [2+2+] additions of X=Y (CH$_{2}$=CH$_{2}$, CH$_{2}$=O and CpO$_{2}$Re=O) with the C=C+ bond of ketene. From the ketene and X=Yhrm{X=Y}$Segment to analyze the electronic structure of the transition state from the perspective of the interaction between segments. Although the reactions of metal oxides and heterocumulenes have the lowest activation barriers, the stabilization from orbital interactions in their transition states is the weakest.

spans.content: Introduction

page_payload.source_text: The concepts developed by Woodward and Hoffmann to classify pericyclic reactions belong to the tools of every chemist.2 However, simple cycloadditions that are of consider- able industrial importance3 challenge these concepts: the thermal [ 2 ~ + ~ 2 ] additions of ketenophiles such as ethylene4 and +formaldehyde5 across the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { C } moiety of ketene.6 Whether a [ 2 \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } + 2 \pi _ { \mathrm { a } } ] topology (Figure 1, left) or a [ 2 \pi \mathrm { _ s } + ( 2 \pi \mathrm { _ s } + 2 \pi \mathrm { _ s } ) ] +topology7 involving the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { O } + + moiety of ketene (Figure 1, right) describes the transition states (TS) best has controversially been discussed.8,9 Theoretically predicted transition structures4,5 and a recent frontier-orbital study8,10 on the reactions give evidence for the latter topology model. Common fragment-based charge- and energy-decomposition schemes11,12 have not yet been used to analyze these reactions. These methods11,12 characterize molecules of the type \mathrm { A } ^ { - } \mathrm { B } in terms of interactions between -the fragments A and B,13 and they should become valuable tools of organic chemists to gain insight into the electronic structure of transition states for cycloadditions.

page_payload.translation_unit_protected_source_text: The concepts developed by Woodward and Hoffmann to classify pericyclic reactions belong to the tools of every chemist.2 However, simple cycloadditions that are of consider- able industrial importance3 challenge these concepts: the thermal [ 2 ~ + ~ 2 ] additions of ketenophiles such as ethylene4 and +formaldehyde5 across the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { C } moiety of ketene.6 Whether a [ 2 \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } + 2 \pi _ { \mathrm { a } } ] topology (Figure 1, left) or a [ 2 \pi \mathrm { _ s } + ( 2 \pi \mathrm { _ s } + 2 \pi \mathrm { _ s } ) ] +topology7 involving the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { O } + + moiety of ketene (Figure 1, right) describes the transition states (TS) best has controversially been discussed.8,9 Theoretically predicted transition structures4,5 and a recent frontier-orbital study8,10 on the reactions give evidence for the latter topology model. Common fragment-based charge- and energy-decomposition schemes11,12 have not yet been used to analyze these reactions. These methods11,12 characterize molecules of the type \mathrm { A } ^ { - } \mathrm { B } in terms of interactions between -the fragments A and B,13 and they should become valuable tools of organic chemists to gain insight into the electronic structure of transition states for cycloadditions.
page_payload.translation_unit_protected_translated_te: The concepts developed by Woodward and Hoffmann for classifying pericyclic reactions have become an essential tool for every chemist. However, simple cycloaddition reactions of industrial importance challenge these concepts: for example, the thermal [2+2] addition reaction between enophiles such as ethylene and formaldehyde and the C=C unit of ketene. Whether the transition state (TS) is best described by the [2$\pi_{\mathrm{s}}$ + 2$\pi_{\mathrm{a}}$] topology (Figure 1 left) or by the [2$\pi_{\mathrm{s}}$ + (2$\pi_{\mathrm{s}}$ + 2$\pi_{\mathrm{s}}$)] topology involving the C=O unit of ketene has been debated. Theoretically predicted transition state structures and recent frontier orbital studies of these reactions provide evidence for the latter topological model. Common charge and energy decomposition schemes based on segments have not yet been used to analyze these reactions. These methods describe through Segmentthe interaction between Segments A and B to describe $\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B}$ type molecules, which should serve as valuable tools for organic chemists to gain deeper understanding of the electronic structure of transition states in cycloaddition reactions.

However, simple cycloaddition reactions of industrial importance challenge these concepts: for example, the thermal [2+2] addition reaction between enophiles such as ethylene and formaldehyde and the C=C unit of ketene. page_payload.source_text: Tremendous research efforts focus on transition-metal oxide -additions across unsaturated carbon carbon bonds,14 since there -is a considerable industrial interest in the activation of double bonds by readily accessible metal oxides, beyond the common methods for cis dihydroxylation using osmium tetraoxide and permanganate.3 Several density-functional studies on these reactions were recently reported because their mechanisms provide some academic challenges.15 23 While transition-metal oxide additions to ethylene follow a [ 3 + 2 ] - pathway only,15-24 cyclopentadienyltrioxorhenium ( \mathrm { V I I } ) ^ { 2 5 } +kinetically favors the [2 + \ 2 ] addition across the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { C } bond of ketene.26 The [ 3 + 2 ] + +activation energies can easily be predicted from frontier-orbital analysis21,22 or even from thermochemical data using Marcus theory.23 However, in our recent computational study, we were unable to rationalize the activation energies of [ 2 ~ + ~ 2 ] additions.22

page_payload.mixed_original_protected_source_text: Tremendous research efforts focus on transition-metal oxide -additions across unsaturated carbon carbon bonds,14 since there -is a considerable industrial interest in the activation of double bonds by readily accessible metal oxides, beyond the common methods for cis dihydroxylation using osmium tetraoxide and permanganate.3 Several density-functional studies on these reactions were recently reported because their mechanisms provide some academic challenges.15 23 While transition-metal oxide additions to ethylene follow a [ 3 + 2 ] - pathway only,15-24 cyclopentadienyltrioxorhenium ( \mathrm { V I I } ) ^ { 2 5 } +kinetically favors the [2 + \ 2 ] addition across the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { C } bond of ketene.26 The [ 3 + 2 ] + +activation energies can easily be predicted from frontier-orbital analysis21,22 or even from thermochemical data using Marcus theory.23 However, in our recent computational study, we were unable to rationalize the activation energies of [ 2 ~ + ~ 2 ] additions.22
page_payload.translation_unit_protected_source_text: Tremendous research efforts focus on transition-metal oxide -additions across unsaturated carbon carbon bonds,14 since there -is a considerable industrial interest in the activation of double bonds by readily accessible metal oxides, beyond the common methods for cis dihydroxylation using osmium tetraoxide and permanganate.3 Several density-functional studies on these reactions were recently reported because their mechanisms provide some academic challenges.15 23 While transition-metal oxide additions to ethylene follow a [ 3 + 2 ] - pathway only,15-24 cyclopentadienyltrioxorhenium ( \mathrm { V I I } ) ^ { 2 5 } +kinetically favors the [2 + \ 2 ] addition across the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { C } bond of ketene.26 The [ 3 + 2 ] + +activation energies can easily be predicted from frontier-orbital analysis21,22 or even from thermochemical data using Marcus theory.23 However, in our recent computational study, we were unable to rationalize the activation energies of [ 2 ~ + ~ 2 ] additions.22
page_payload.translation_unit_protected_translated_te: A large body of research has focused on addition reactions of transition metal oxides to unsaturated carbon-carbon bonds,$^{14}$ because apart from the conventional methods using osmium tetroxide and permanganate for cis-dihydroxylation,$^{3}$ the activation of double bonds using readily available metal oxides has considerable industrial significance. Several density functional studies of these reactions have been reported recently, as their mechanisms pose some academic challenges.$^{15-23}$ While addition reactions of transition metal oxides with ethylene follow only the [3+2] pathway,$^{15-24}$ cyclopentadienyl trioxorhenium ($\mathrm{VII}$)$^{25}$ kinetically prefers the [2+2] addition with the C=C bond of ketene.$^{26}$ The activation energy of [3+2] reactions can be readily predicted through frontier orbital analysis$^{21,22}$ or even using thermodynamic data from Marcus theory.$^{23}$ However, in our recent computational study, we could not rationalize the activation energy of [2+2] addition reactions.$^{22}$
page_payload.translation_unit_translated_text: A large body of research has focused on addition reactions of transition metal oxides to unsaturated carbon-carbon bonds,$^{14}$ because apart from the conventional methods using osmium tetroxide and permanganate for cis-dihydroxylation,$^{3}$ the activation of double bonds using readily available metal oxides has considerable industrial significance. Several density functional studies of these reactions have been reported recently, as their mechanisms pose some academic challenges.$^{15-23}$ While addition reactions of transition metal oxides with ethylene follow only the [3+2] pathway,$^{15-24}$ cyclopentadienyl trioxorhenium ($\mathrm{VII}$)$^{25}$ kinetically prefers the [2+2] addition with the C=C bond of ketene.$^{26}$ The activation energy of [3+2] reactions can be readily predicted through frontier orbital analysis$^{21,22}$ or even using thermodynamic data from Marcus theory.$^{23}$ However, in our recent computational study, we could not rationalize the activation energy of [2+2] addition reactions.$^{22}$
page_payload.protected_translated_text: A large body of research has focused on addition reactions of transition metal oxides to unsaturated carbon-carbon bonds,$^{14}$ because apart from the conventional methods using osmium tetroxide and permanganate for cis-dihydroxylation,$^{3}$ the activation of double bonds using readily available metal oxides has considerable industrial significance. Several density functional studies of these reactions have been reported recently, as their mechanisms pose some academic challenges.$^{15-23}$ While addition reactions of transition metal oxides with ethylene follow only the [3+2] pathway,$^{15-24}$ cyclopentadienyl trioxorhenium ($\mathrm{VII}$)$^{25}$ kinetically prefers the [2+2] addition with the C=C bond of ketene.$^{26}$ The activation energy of [3+2] reactions can be readily predicted through frontier orbital analysis$^{21,22}$ or even using thermodynamic data from Marcus theory.$^{23}$ However, in our recent computational study, we could not rationalize the activation energy of [2+2] addition reactions.$^{22}$
page_payload.translated_text: A large body of research has focused on addition reactions of transition metal oxides to unsaturated carbon-carbon bonds,$^{14}$ because apart from the conventional methods using osmium tetroxide and permanganate for cis-dihydroxylation,$^{3}$ the activation of double bonds using readily available metal oxides has considerable industrial significance. Several density functional studies of these reactions have been reported recently, as their mechanisms pose some academic challenges.$^{15-23}$ While addition reactions of transition metal oxides with ethylene follow only the [3+2] pathway,$^{15-24}$ cyclopentadienyl trioxorhenium ($\mathrm{VII}$)$^{25}$ kinetically prefers the [2+2] addition with the C=C bond of ketene.$^{26}$ The activation energy of [3+2] reactions can be readily predicted through frontier orbital analysis$^{21,22}$ or even using thermodynamic data from Marcus theory.$^{23}$ However, in our recent computational study, we could not rationalize the activation energy of [2+2] addition reactions.$^{22}$

page_payload.source_text: We have performed a comparative density-functional (DFT) study on the electronic structure of the transition states for the addition of \mathrm { X = Y } \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } { = } \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } , \mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } { = } \mathrm { O } , and { \mathrm { C p R e O } } _ { 3 } ) across the \mathrm { C } { = } \mathrm { C } moiety of ketene. We use two state-of-the-art decomposition schemes based on the interactions between the \mathrm { X = Y } and ketene fragments in the TS: charge decomposition analysis (CDA)11 and the Extended Transition State (ETS) method.12

We used two advanced decomposition schemes based on the interaction between X=Y segments and ketene segments in the transition state: Charge Decomposition Analysis (CDA)$^{11}$ and the Extended Transition State (ETS) method.$^{12}$

We used two advanced decomposition schemes based on the interaction between X=Y segments and ketene segments in the transition state: Charge Decomposition Analysis (CDA)$^{11}$ and the Extended Transition State (ETS) method.$^{12}$

page_payload.translated_text: We conducted a comparative density functional (DFT) study of the electronic structure of transition states for additions of X=Y (CH$_2$=CH$_2$, CH$_2$=O and CpReO$_3$) to the C=C portion of ketene. We used two advanced decomposition schemes based on the interaction between X=Y segments and ketene segments in the transition state: Charge Decomposition Analysis (CDA)$^{11}$ and the Extended Transition State (ETS) method.$^{12}$

spans.content: Methods

page_payload.source_text: Geometry Optimization and Energy Calculation. The molecules and transition states were optimized at the gradient- corrected DFT level using the exchange functional of Becke27 and the correlation functional of Perdew28 (BP86). Relativistic effects were considered using the zeroth-order regular ap- proximation (ZORA).29 Uncontracted Slater-type orbitals (STOs) were used as basis functions for the SCF calculations.30 The basis functions at the metal have triple- \xi quality, augmented with a set of 6p functions. The basis set at the other atoms has double- \xi quality, augmented with a set of d-type polarization functions. The (1s)2 core electrons of C and O and the \mathrm { ( 1 s 2 s 2 p 3 s 3 p 3 d 4 s 4 p 4 d ) ^ { 4 6 } } inner shells of Re were treated within the frozen-core approximation.31 An auxiliary basis set of s, p, d, f, and g STOs was utilized to fit the molecular densities and

page_payload.translation_unit_protected_source_text: Geometry Optimization and Energy Calculation. The molecules and transition states were optimized at the gradient- corrected DFT level using the exchange functional of Becke27 and the correlation functional of Perdew28 (BP86). Relativistic effects were considered using the zeroth-order regular ap- proximation (ZORA).29 Uncontracted Slater-type orbitals (STOs) were used as basis functions for the SCF calculations.30 The basis functions at the metal have triple- \xi quality, augmented with a set of 6p functions. The basis set at the other atoms has double- \xi quality, augmented with a set of d-type polarization functions. The (1s)2 core electrons of C and O and the \mathrm { ( 1 s 2 s 2 p 3 s 3 p 3 d 4 s 4 p 4 d ) ^ { 4 6 } } inner shells of Re were treated within the frozen-core approximation.31 An auxiliary basis set of s, p, d, f, and g STOs was utilized to fit the molecular densities and to represent the Coulomb and exchange potentials in each SCF cycle.32 This basis-set combination is denoted \mathrm { I I I } \sim . 22,23 Unless otherwise stated, energies reported refer to the BP86/III∼ level. The calculations were carried out using the ADF 2000 program package.34

Relativistic effects were considered using the zeroth-order regular ap- proximation (ZORA).29 Uncontracted Slater-type orbitals (STOs) were used as basis functions for the SCF calculations.30 The basis functions at the metal have triple- \xi quality, augmented with a set of 6p functions. replay_result.translated_text: Geometry optimization and energy calculations. Molecules and transition states were optimized at the gradient-corrected density functional theory (DFT) level, using Becke's exchange functional27 and Perdew's correlation functional28 (BP86). Relativistic effects were treated using the zeroth-order regular approximation (ZORA)29. Self-consistent field (SCF) calculations used uncontracted Slater-type orbitals (STOs) as basis functions30. The basis functions at the metal atoms have triple-$\xi$ quality, augmented with a set of 6p functions. The basis set at other atoms has double-$\xi$ quality, augmented with a set of d-type polarization functions. The (1s)$^2$ core electrons of C and O and the $\mathrm{(1s2s2p3s3p3d4s4p4d)^{46}}$ inner shells of Re were treated within the frozen-core approximation$^{31}$. An auxiliary basis set of s, p, d, f, and g type STOs was utilized to fit the molecular densities and represent the Coulomb and exchange potentials in each SCF cycle$^{32}$. This basis-set combination is denoted as $\mathrm{III}\sim$$^{22,23}$. Unless otherwise stated, the reported energies refer to the BP86/III~ level. Calculations were carried out using the ADF 2000 program package$^{34}$.
